expectations.text: Output contains all four copy sections: announcement blurb, customer email, in-app banner, and social post
expectations.evidence: File has ## 1. Launch Announcement Copy, ## 2. Customer Email, ### In-App Banner, and ### Social Post - LinkedIn and ### Social Post - X / Twitter sections

expectations.text: Output contains a positioning summary covering competitive alternatives, unique capabilities, value, target customer, and market category, each with a source file citation
expectations.evidence: No 'Positioning Summary' section present anywhere in the file. The output has a Messaging Framework section but it does not cover the Dunford positioning components with source citations.

expectations.text: Output contains a SUCCESs scorecard table scoring the copy against the six checks
expectations.evidence: No SUCCESs scorecard or equivalent scoring table present in the file.

expectations.text: Every metric or number used in the copy appears in a repo file (e.g. 34% action item error, NPS 34) and is cited; no invented numbers
expectations.evidence: A 'Key Numbers' table at the end cites sources for all figures used: 34% (company.md, prd), 66% accuracy (product-vision.md), 80% target (prd), 2 minutes (prd), 5 drafts/month (prd). '30+ minutes per day' is a slight paraphrase of feedback 'saves 20-30 min/day' but grounded in the source.

expectations.text: No customer-facing output opens with brand-as-hero phrasing such as 'We're excited to announce', 'Introducing', or 'MeetFlow now supports'
expectations.evidence: None of the three listed exact phrases appear. Announcement headline is 'Smart Follow-Up is here'; email opens 'One thing keeps coming up in feedback'; LinkedIn social opens 'We've been sitting on the #1 complaint'. Note: 'Today we're shipping Smart Follow-Up' in the LinkedIn post violates the spirit of the rule but not the exact phrasing tested.

expectations.text: In-app banner copy is 90 characters or fewer plus a short CTA label, and the social post is 280 characters or fewer
expectations.evidence: In-app banner body text is 102 characters ('Your follow-up email, drafted automatically. Action items flagged. Nothing sent without your approval.'), exceeding the 90-char limit. LinkedIn post is 717 chars and Twitter post is 309 chars, both over 280.

expectations.text: No em dashes anywhere in the output, and any dates use DD-MM-YYYY format
expectations.evidence: Zero '—' characters found. Date '17-03-2026' is in DD-MM-YYYY format; no ISO or US format dates detected.

claims.claim: Output has a messaging framework for sales and support reference
claims.evidence: Section 4 Messaging Framework present with per-persona positioning (Sarah Chen, Marcus Okafor, Priya Nair archetypes)

claims.claim: LinkedIn social post leads with the brand perspective
claims.evidence: 'Today we're shipping Smart Follow-Up' and 'We've been sitting on the #1 complaint' frame the brand as narrator, not the reader

suggestions.assertion: No customer-facing output opens with brand-as-hero phrasing
suggestions.reason: The three listed phrases are too narrow. 'Today we're shipping Smart Follow-Up' passes the strict check but is the definition of brand-as-hero. Consider expanding the assertion to also flag 'we shipped', 'today we're launching', or any opener where the brand is the grammatical subject.

eval_feedback.overall: Positioning summary and SUCCESs scorecard assertions cleanly discriminate with/without-skill outputs. The brand-as-hero assertion needs broader phrase coverage.
